caveats: a: No public six-digit occupation mix or wage-weighted task study exists for this heterogeneous industry.
a: The broad maintenance occupation does not capture the exact mix of pool, chimney, duct, drain, gutter, exterior-cleaning, and snow services.
a: The supplied compensation ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts.
rho: General SME adoption does not measure labor implementation in property-service firms.
rho: Marketing or message generation may increase activity without reducing labor requirements.
rho: Service-line differences in route density, recordkeeping, seasonality, and safety create wide implementation variance.
e: No source measures lens eligibility within the frozen EBITDA band.
e: Annual or multi-year recommended maintenance is repeatable but not necessarily contractually recurring.
e: The estimated firm count may mix materially different service lines and margin structures.
s5: Gallup covers all employer businesses rather than this industry or EBITDA band.
s5: Owner intentions are not completed transactions and include potentially nonqualifying transfers.
s5: Transferability likely differs sharply between dense pool routes and owner-expert inspection or project businesses.
q: No public source reports the national revenue-weighted pricing mix for NAICS 561790.
q: The direct billing evidence covers pool service and may not generalize to project-oriented subsegments.
q: Efficiency has no retained value unless paid hours fall, hiring is avoided, or released capacity can be sold.
d5: Occupational employment is not industry service quantity and includes work outside NAICS 561790.
d5: Maintenance recommendations do not prove customers purchase the recommended service or outsource it.
d5: The broad code combines weekly routes, annual or multi-year inspections, discretionary cleaning, emergencies, and weather-dependent services.
o: No source directly measures operator-required quantity for the combined industry.
o: Residential customers can self-clean pools, gutters, drains, and exteriors despite safety or quality tradeoffs.
o: Robotic pool cleaners, remote monitoring, and bundled facility services may erode specialist demand unevenly.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External homeowners, property managers, associations, businesses, institutions, and public facilities buy weekly or monthly routes, seasonal programs, annual inspections, periodic maintenance, and one-time cleaning or corrective projects. Billing may be flat monthly, per visit, per asset, hourly, or fixed by quoted scope, with chemical and repair materials either bundled or separately charged.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive facilities staff; pest control, janitorial, landscaping, carpet cleaning, construction, licensed-trade installation, or remediation businesses properly classified elsewhere; software-only monitoring; one-person practices without a transferable operating organization; shells; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms repeatedly providing outsourced physical services to buildings and dwellings, including swimming-pool cleaning and maintenance, chimney cleaning and inspection, ventilation-duct cleaning, drain or gutter cleaning, pressure washing and other building-exterior cleaning, commercial kitchen exhaust cleaning, parking-lot cleaning, lighting maintenance, and stand-alone driveway or parking-lot snow removal.

a.bridge: BLS describes general maintenance and repair workers across many industries rather than the occupation mix and wage-weighted tasks of NAICS 561790, while SBA describes generic small-business AI uses. I map on-site cleaning, climbing, inspection, chemical testing, equipment operation, preventive service, diagnosis, and repair to low exposure; I map customer intake, reminders, route support, estimate drafting, record summarization, marketing, purchasing administration, and routine communication to higher exposure. General maintenance includes repairs outside this industry's scope and may overstate diagnostic work, while the industry's pool routes may contain more standardized recordkeeping than the proxy reveals.
caveats: No public six-digit occupation mix or wage-weighted task study exists for this heterogeneous industry.
The broad maintenance occupation does not capture the exact mix of pool, chimney, duct, drain, gutter, exterior-cleaning, and snow services.
The supplied compensation ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts.
a.change_evidence: A representative NAICS 561790 payroll-weighted time study split by service line and role, measuring field execution, travel, inspection, repair, dispatch, sales, administration, current software automation, and validated AI feasibility, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: The Census service basket is dominated by work on physical property. BLS describes maintenance workers inspecting and diagnosing problems, performing preventive service, ordering supplies, estimating costs, keeping records, and using tools at varied sites; only the information and coordination portions are strongly exposed to current AI. SBA identifies repeat administration, customer service, content, and business-data analysis as practical small-business uses. [S1, S2, S3]

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment for general maintenance and repair workers across industries rather than constant-price service quantity for NAICS 561790. CDC, CSIA, and NADCA document recurring maintenance needs for selected pool, chimney, and duct services but do not measure market growth or the other included lines. I translate BLS's 4 percent decade employment growth and continuing need for older-property maintenance into modest five-year quantity growth, then widen for housing and commercial occupancy, pool stock, climate and snow variability, construction cycles, customer deferral, self-service, and service-line mix. Employment can understate quantity when equipment raises productivity, while general maintenance and safety guidance can overstate paid outsourcing.
caveats: Occupational employment is not industry service quantity and includes work outside NAICS 561790.
Maintenance recommendations do not prove customers purchase the recommended service or outsource it.
The broad code combines weekly routes, annual or multi-year inspections, discretionary cleaning, emergencies, and weather-dependent services.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price Census revenue by detailed 561790 product, combined with route counts, installed pool and fireplace bases, building age, service frequency, backlog, customer churn, and climate-normalized snow and exterior-cleaning volumes, would materially change the year-five ratio.
d5.rationale: BLS expects 4 percent growth from 2024 to 2034 for general maintenance and says older residential and nonresidential structures require more maintenance. CDC calls for frequent pool testing and preventive maintenance; CSIA cites annual chimney inspection guidance; NADCA recommends annual HVAC inspection and cleaning as needed, historically around every four to five years. These recurring physical needs support broadly stable to modestly growing demand, offset by cyclicality, weather, and deferrable household projects. [S1, S2, S7, S8, S9]

caveats: No source measures lens eligibility within the frozen EBITDA band.
Annual or multi-year recommended maintenance is repeatable but not necessarily contractually recurring.
The estimated firm count may mix materially different service lines and margin structures.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level diligence across the supplied population showing revenue by service line, repeat-customer and contracted-route share, customer concentration, owner field hours, crew depth, licenses or certifications, and transferable agreements would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is inherently external property service, and each major line can produce repeat demand: pool care can be weekly, chimneys and HVAC systems are periodically inspected, and gutters, drains, exteriors, lots, and snow routes recur seasonally or as conditions return. Firms in the supplied EBITDA band are more likely to have crews, routes, equipment, supervisors, and transferable customer records. I exclude businesses dominated by isolated projects, misclassified construction or remediation, captive operations, and owner-personal delivery without organizational continuity. [S1, S6, S7, S8, S9]

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 561790 ($5,789,061,076) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 561790 ($13,714,624,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $156,878 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561790); class <5: 12,845 firms, avg revenue/firm $312,420 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $48,894 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 2,529 firms, avg revenue/firm $931,722 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $145,814 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,248 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,866,649 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $292,131 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 643 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,766,753 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $745,997 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 54 firms, avg revenue/firm $17,978,148 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,813,580 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 23 firms, avg revenue/firm $42,602,174 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,667,240 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 77

o.bridge: Census defines a physical service basket, BLS describes maintenance tasks, and CDC, CSIA, and NADCA specify operator activities for selected lines; none measures the share of year-five demand requiring an accountable outsourced firm. I map on-site inspection, testing, chemical handling, climbing, confined access, pressure washing, extraction, sweeping or plowing, diagnosis, preventive maintenance, and corrective work to operator-required quantity, while allowing self-service, sensors, robotic pool equipment, improved materials, bundled facilities providers, and customer in-house staff to displace some specialist demand. Safety-oriented institutional sources may overstate professional outsourcing relative to residential behavior.
caveats: No source directly measures operator-required quantity for the combined industry.
Residential customers can self-clean pools, gutters, drains, and exteriors despite safety or quality tradeoffs.
Robotic pool cleaners, remote monitoring, and bundled facility services may erode specialist demand unevenly.
o.change_evidence: A revenue-weighted customer study by service line tracking scopes retained with specialist firms, moved in-house, bundled into facilities contracts, self-served with equipment or sensors, automated, or eliminated would materially change operator-required share.
o.rationale: Most purchased outcomes require access to customer property and physical work with tools, chemicals, vehicles, or specialized equipment. CDC requires trained pool operation and frequent testing; CSIA describes complete chimney sweeping and inspection standards; NADCA defines whole-system duct cleaning. AI can support diagnosis and records but cannot deliver most field outcomes, although customers can self-serve or shift to bundled providers and more automated equipment. [S1, S2, S7, S8, S9]

q.bridge: PoolDial describes pool-service billing rather than a revenue-weighted contract mix across all NAICS 561790 service lines, while BLS and SBA identify estimation, records, and administrative tasks without measuring captured benefits. I assign relatively high initial retention to flat monthly pool routes and fixed quoted maintenance or cleaning projects, lower retention to hourly, per-visit, cost-plus-material, and competitively rebid work, then discount for renewals, customer shopping, seasonal price pressure, competitor adoption, and service-level improvements. Pool-service evidence may overstate recurring fixed-price prevalence for chimney, duct, gutter, pressure-washing, drain, and snow work.
caveats: No public source reports the national revenue-weighted pricing mix for NAICS 561790.
The direct billing evidence covers pool service and may not generalize to project-oriented subsegments.
Efficiency has no retained value unless paid hours fall, hiring is avoided, or released capacity can be sold.
q.change_evidence: A representative invoice and contract audit by 561790 service line showing revenue by pricing basis, material pass-through, renewal cadence, price changes after automation, gross margin, route density, and customer churn would materially change retention.
q.rationale: PoolDial describes flat monthly billing as standard for weekly pool service, with per-visit billing used for one-time work. Fixed monthly and quoted-scope revenue can initially preserve administrative and routing savings, whereas per-visit, hourly, material-sensitive, and rebid work passes more value to customers. Local competition and easy switching dissipate a meaningful share within five years. [S2, S3, S6]

rho.bridge: OECD measures AI adoption across SMEs in multiple sectors and countries, and SBA lists generic use cases rather than production implementation in NAICS 561790. I translate low current core-process adoption and mostly peripheral SME use into five-year implementation of this industry's exposed administrative opportunity, allowing faster uptake through embedded field-service tools but discounting fragmented data, seasonal workflows, owner attention, integration costs, technician training, and human review of safety- or property-related outputs. OECD's firms with at least 10 employees may be more digitally mature than local home-service operators, biasing implementation upward; rapid software embedding could bias it downward.
caveats: General SME adoption does not measure labor implementation in property-service firms.
Marketing or message generation may increase activity without reducing labor requirements.
Service-line differences in route density, recordkeeping, seasonality, and safety create wide implementation variance.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year panel of LMM firms across the major 561790 service lines measuring tool deployment, workflow coverage, accepted-output rates, office and field hours, rework, integration, and avoided hiring would materially change implementation.
rho.rationale: AI can be implemented in intake, scheduling, route planning support, draft estimates, customer messages, marketing, documentation, and basic trend analysis without automating physical work. OECD reports that 11.9 percent of 10-49 employee firms used AI in 2024, core-function adoption remained below 10 percent in G7 countries, and SME generative-AI use was mostly peripheral. SBA advises firms to start small and retain human review. [S3, S4]

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year intentions among US employer-business owners across industries, not completed control transfers of eligible LMM firms in NAICS 561790. I anchor below its 22 percent employer sell-or-transfer intention after excluding plans that never close, gifts or internal succession that may not qualify, non-control events, firms outside the band, and operations that wind down with the owner. Route-based pool or seasonal businesses may be more transferable than the general population, while certification, local reputation, and owner technical dependence may reduce transferability in chimney, duct, or specialty cleaning firms.
caveats: Gallup covers all employer businesses rather than this industry or EBITDA band.
Owner intentions are not completed transactions and include potentially nonqualifying transfers.
Transferability likely differs sharply between dense pool routes and owner-expert inspection or project businesses.
s5.change_evidence: A transaction census for NAICS 561790 linking private and announced control changes to the eligible denominator, with service line, seller EBITDA, buyer type, ownership percentage, and post-close customer and technician retention, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that 22 percent of employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or go public within five years and 5 percent planned to close. Firms with documented routes, technicians, equipment, and recurring accounts can transfer, but intentions overstate completed qualifying control changes. [S5]

principal_driver: Recurring physical maintenance needs and fixed monthly or quoted pricing create a modest, retainable opportunity to automate coordination around field work.
principal_weakness: Most labor is inseparable from on-site physical execution, while the broad service mix makes the estimated acquisition pool and average economics unusually uncertain.

run_meta.title: Other Services to Buildings and Dwellings

sources.supports: Defines the industry and lists building-exterior cleaning, swimming-pool cleaning and maintenance, chimney cleaning, ventilation-duct cleaning, and drain or gutter cleaning as illustrative examples.
sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual: 561790 Other Services to Buildings and Dwellings

sources.supports: Describes physical inspection, diagnosis, preventive maintenance, estimating, recordkeeping, tool use, varied-site work, and projects 4 percent employment growth from 2024 to 2034 with continuing need at older properties.
sources.title: General Maintenance and Repair Workers

sources.supports: Identifies repeat administration, data analysis, content, customer service, phone routing, and review responses as small-business AI uses while recommending small starts, human review, and data safeguards.
sources.title: AI for Small Business

sources.supports: Reports lower adoption among SMEs, 11.9 percent use among 10-49 employee firms in 2024, below-10-percent core-business adoption in G7 countries, and mostly peripheral generative-AI use among SMEs.
sources.title: AI Adoption by Small and Medium-Sized Enterprises

sources.supports: Reports that 22 percent of US employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or go public within five years and 5 percent planned to close.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Describes flat monthly billing as standard for weekly pool service, autopay and recurring revenue, and per-visit billing for one-time repairs, cleanups, openings, and closings.
sources.title: Monthly vs Per-Visit Billing for Pool Service

sources.supports: Calls for trained pool operation, at-least-twice-daily water testing, cleaning, records, filtration maintenance, shock treatment, and preventive equipment maintenance.
sources.title: Operating and Managing Public Pools, Hot Tubs and Splash Pads

sources.supports: Cites NFPA 211 guidance that chimneys, fireplaces, and vents be inspected at least annually and describes complete chimney sweeping and Level 1 inspection.
sources.title: Chimney Safety Institute of America Homeowner Resources

sources.supports: Recommends annual HVAC-system inspection, complete cleaning historically every four to five years subject to conditions, and defines complete system cleaning under NADCA's ACR standard.
sources.title: Prepare HVAC Systems for Fall Season

title: Other Services to Buildings and Dwellings
